metrics.narrative: On the 3 Apr 2026 iRisk demo, the buyer's stated pain was reactive risk management: they want to 'proactively identify supplier risks to avoid late awareness of critical events' and to automatically block invoice payments to sanctioned suppliers to 'reduce compliance risk.' They also flagged the value of a combined internal-score plus third-party-data plus AI-radar dashboard for visibility. None of this was ever converted into a number (no stated cost of a missed sanctions event, no target reduction in manual review hours, no headcount or cycle-time figure). The value case remains qualitative.

champion.narrative: No champion has developed for iRisk. The Procurement COE Director has been the constant thread from the 23 Jan 2026 intro call through the 28 Apr 2026 P2P discussion, but his actions have been process-neutral rather than advocacy-driven: he asked Zycus to strip iRisk out of the bundled CLM pricing (7 Apr 2026) rather than defend keeping it in scope, and there is no record of him pushing the deal internally, introducing an economic buyer, or replying to the rep's 26 May 2026 check-in. This reads as an engaged-but-passive coach at best, not a champion.

Avoma: Zycus Proposed CLM POC Framework Review for Cadence Design, 28 Apr 2026
competition.narrative: No competitor has been named specifically against iRisk. Coupa is the incumbent CLM tool at Cadence and the named rival on the sibling CLM opportunity, where the buyer told Zycus outright they are running 'a similar sandbox with the other candidate solution' with fewer constraints (28 Apr 2026) — but that comment was in the CLM context, not iRisk. Given the same buying committee runs both evaluations, it would be reasonable to expect a supplier-risk module comparison exists somewhere in their process, but Zycus has no visibility into it for iRisk specifically. This absence of information is itself a risk: Zycus cannot counter a threat it cannot see.

identify_pain.narrative: The 3 Apr 2026 iRisk demo surfaced concrete operational pain: no automated alerting on supplier risk events, no system-driven payment blocking when a supplier hits a sanctions list, and no way today to import PO/ERP spend data into a risk-impact view. The buyer also asked how corrective action plans get closed out with underperforming suppliers, implying today's process is manual and reactive. The pain owners were the supply-chain and procurement attendees on that call, though individual name attribution to specific quotes is not independently verifiable beyond the Procurement COE Director's continued involvement across calls.

sources: Avoma: Zycus CLM Workshop - Follow Up Session, 21 Apr 2026
paper_process.narrative: No paper process specific to iRisk has surfaced. A Zycus master services agreement was raised for the sibling CLM deal (21 Apr 2026, to 'kick off parallel legal review'), but there is no evidence it was scoped to cover iRisk or that legal/procurement has looked at iRisk contracting terms at all. Visibility would come from confirming whether the CLM MSA is intended to be the umbrella agreement for iRisk too, or whether a separate Order Form is required.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has surfaced on the iRisk track. The Procurement COE Director (per Avoma, 'Devesh Chadha') has been the account's steady point of contact since the 23 Jan 2026 intro call and is described there as overseeing 'global processes, policy, technology, analytics, and data' for procurement, which gives him process influence but no confirmed budget sign-off authority on a $65k module purchase. No VP-level sponsor has appeared on any iRisk call. Ten-plus weeks into Formal Evaluation with 24 days to close, this is a live disqualification risk.

decision_process.narrative: iRisk began as an add-on inside the bundled CLM proposal, then was explicitly pulled out at the buyer's request on 7 Apr 2026 so it could be priced and evaluated on its own. That split produced a dedicated 'iRisk Commercial Proposal Review' on 21 May 2026, but the meeting left no recorded content, so the process step it represented (a commercial walkthrough, a scoring readout, or something else) is unconfirmed. No process map beyond that exists; the parallel CLM opportunity on the same account has since advanced to Shortlisted while iRisk has not moved stage.

decision_criteria.narrative: On the iRisk demo the buyer's questions clustered around integration depth (SAP backend connectivity, 'how does it go from somebody requesting a supplier all the way to going to SAP'), breadth of third-party risk data sources ('can we add a source to the list?'), automated escalation on non-response, and a roadmap question on sub-tier supplier mapping. These signal the buyer is evaluating integration completeness and data-source coverage as key criteria, but no formal scorecard or weighted RFP criteria for iRisk specifically has been shared with Zycus (the rep's own Next Step confirms 'plan to learn what their scoring criteria is').

day_summary.overall: The only signal today was the close date sliding a full month, from end-of-June to end-of-July — a quiet admission that this deal wasn't going to land on the original timeline. No accompanying email or call activity, so the reason for the push isn't documented here; likely reflects stalled progress or a buyer-side delay that hasn't been captured yet. Ball's effectively still with us to find out why and re-anchor the plan.

flags: No economic buyer mapped or engaged after 10 weeks in Formal Evaluation — a $65k enterprise CLM deal will not close without CFO/CPO sign-off. The move: demand a direct CFO/CPO/CIO call in the re-engagement conversation to map the economic buyer and validate budget authority. If the buyer refuses or delays, disqualify the deal.

No champion — the procurement COE contact engaged but never advocated (no EB intro, no internal timeline shared post-28 Apr, no reply to the 26 May check-in for 6 weeks). Without a champion, a silent deal will not re-engage. The move: in the re-engagement call, ask the contact directly whether they are championing Zycus internally and whether they can introduce the economic buyer. If not, pivot to finding a new champion (e.g. a legal or IT stakeholder from the April workshops) or disqualify.

No competitive intelligence — the buyer is evaluating Zycus alongside an unnamed competitor who offered a more flexible POC, and Zycus does not know if the competitor already won. The move: in the re-engagement call, ask the buyer directly 'Who else is in the final evaluation? Where do we stand vs. the other vendor? Has a decision been made?' If the buyer is transparent, Zycus can counter-position; if the buyer is evasive, the deal is likely lost.

Zero OpportunityContactRole records — the buyer's key contacts (procurement COE director, IT, legal) are known from the calls but are not logged in Salesforce. Add the contacts as OpportunityContactRoles with their titles and roles (e.g. procurement COE director = Influencer, IT lead = Technical Buyer) to enable multi-threading and handoff.
Single-threaded into procurement COE contact with no executive sponsor, no champion, and no multi-level stakeholder coverage. Enterprise deals require broad organizational alignment.

No quantified ROI case. Buyer described operational pain (manual renewals, disparate intake, no metadata capture) but stated no cost-per-contract, headcount savings, or cycle-time improvement target. CFO/CPO will not approve a $65k investment without a financial business case.

Multi-thread beyond the single Procurement COE contact now, before re-engaging, since the account is simultaneously running a more resourced rival evaluation on the parallel CLM deal with the same committee.

Build a quantified ROI story (cost of a missed sanctions event, hours saved on manual escalation) before the next buyer conversation; the current pitch is feature-led with no numbers a budget owner could act on.

Use the sibling CLM opportunity's active Shortlisted-stage momentum as the entry point to re-open the iRisk conversation, since a fresh case on the CLM track may re-surface the buyer's iRisk decision-maker.

The move: in the re-engagement call, ask the buyer directly 'Who else is in the final evaluation? Where do we stand vs. the other vendor? Has a decision been made?' value.name: Coupa
value.quote: Currently using Coupa for CLM; seeking one platform to manage both buy-side vendor contracts and sell-side legal/sales agreements.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 25 Feb 2026
value.how_we_win: Coupa is the incumbent for buy-side only; Cadence needs a unified platform for buy-side, sell-side, and legal contracts, which Coupa does not offer. Zycus's advantage is the single-platform story (iContract CLM + intake orchestration + iRisk supplier risk) serving all three use cases. The wedge is AI + agentic capabilities (Merlin intake agent, AI-driven clause generation, risk analytics) that the buyer explicitly asked for and that Coupa lacks. Activate by positioning Zycus as the only vendor that consolidates procurement, legal, and sales CLM + supplier risk in one AI-native platform.
